title: The 9 AM Alert
setup: You just clocked in. PagerDuty screams: production database latency is spiking and checkout is failing.

A.text: SSH into the server and inspect running processes and slow query logs.
A.result: You have the mindset of an SRE / Infrastructure Engineer—calm, systems-level thinking under pressure.

B.text: Revert the last code deployment immediately via the CI pipeline.
B.result: You think like a Backend Software Engineer—code-first, rapid mitigation.

C.text: Pull the real-time transaction dataset to see if the spike correlates with a specific region or feature.
C.result: You have a Data Analyst / Data Engineer instinct—evidence before action.

title: The Bad Migration
setup: Your investigation reveals a teammate's migration script added a missing index and locked the orders table. Rollback isn't automatic.
output: Locked table: orders | Migration v2.4 | Lock duration: 14m
prompt: The table is still locked. How do you proceed?

A.text: Manually kill the migration process and execute a reverse SQL script.
A.result: You're a DBA / DevOps Engineer—hands-on with data integrity and deployment pipelines.

B.text: Ship an emergency hotfix in the API to queue orders in memory and bypass the DB temporarily.
B.result: You're a Software Engineer—creative workaround, prioritizing user experience.

C.text: Build a quick internal dashboard tracking which orders are stuck so support can reach out.
C.result: You're a Product Operations / Data Engineer—visibility and coordination matter most.

title: Customer Complaints Flood In
setup: Orders went through twice or not at all. Support channels are exploding. The CTO demands an ETA in 15 minutes.
prompt: The pressure is public. What's your move?

A.text: Draft a transparent status page and live incident timeline for users.
A.result: You're a Frontend Engineer / DevRel—communication and user trust are your tools.

B.text: Jump into the war room, assign owners, and coordinate engineering, support, and legal in real time.
B.result: You're an Engineering Manager / Technical Program Manager—systems of people, not just code.

C.text: Run a rapid SQL analysis to quantify exactly how many users and dollars are affected.
C.result: You're a Data Scientist / Analyst—metrics drive every decision.

title: The Cache Lies
setup: Your status page or data report is serving stale numbers. The root cause is a distributed caching layer out of sync with the DB.
output: Cache hit rate: 99.9% | Stale key: order_status | TTL: 300s
prompt: Users are seeing incorrect recovery messages. How do you fix the truth?

A.text: Flush the cache cluster, verify consistency with DB replicas, and deploy a cache-bypass flag.
A.result: You're a Backend / Distributed Systems Engineer—deep knowledge of infrastructure state.

B.text: Redesign the status page UI to clearly distinguish 'pending verification' from 'resolved'.
B.result: You're a UX / Frontend Engineer—design solves ambiguity when data is messy.

C.text: Write an audit script that compares cache vs. database values every 30 seconds and alerts on divergence.
C.result: You're a Data Engineer / QA Automation specialist—testing and observability first.

title: Security Flags the Logs
setup: In the error logs you just flushed, there are raw customer emails and partial card tokens. Security wants it contained now.
output: Leaked fields: email, last4 | Log files: 14 | Retention: 7 days
prompt: There's a potential data exposure in your logs. What's your instinct?

A.text: Sanitize the log pipeline, rotate keys, and write a patch to mask PII at ingestion.
A.result: You're a Security Engineer—protecting users is non-negotiable.

B.text: Document the breach timeline, affected users, and remediation steps for compliance.
B.result: You're a Technical Program Manager / Compliance Analyst—process and accountability matter.

C.text: Build an automated scanner that detects PII patterns in logs before they hit storage.
C.result: You're an ML / Automation Engineer—prevent the next one with intelligence.

title: The 5 PM Post-Mortem
setup: Security is contained. Leadership needs a root-cause analysis with visual evidence delivered before end of day.
output: Incident duration: 4h 12m | Affected users: 1,204 | Root cause: missing index + cache invalidation failure
prompt: You have two hours to make the complex failure understandable. What's your approach?

A.text: Build an interactive timeline dashboard with graphs linking latency, errors, and deployments.
A.result: You're a Data Visualization / BI Engineer—stories are told through data.

B.text: Write a concise, narrative technical report with clear sections: timeline, root cause, fixes, prevention.
B.result: You're a Technical Writer / Senior Engineer—clarity and documentation are leadership tools.

C.text: Prepare a live demo replaying the fix in a staging environment for the CTO.
C.result: You're a DevRel / Solutions Engineer—showing, not telling, builds trust.

title: The Architecture Pattern
setup: Your analysis reveals this isn't a one-off. A single DB dependency is cascading failures across three microservices.
output: Dependency graph: orders -> payments -> notifications | Single point: legacy DB v1
prompt: This crisis reveals a deeper structural risk. How do you respond?

A.text: Propose a phased migration to decouple services with event queues and separate data stores.
A.result: You're a Systems Architect / Senior Backend Engineer—designing for resilience.

B.text: Recommend restructuring the team into dedicated service squads with clear ownership boundaries.
B.result: You're an Engineering Manager / Organizational Designer—scaling people to match architecture.

C.text: Suggest training an anomaly detection model on dependency latency to predict cascades.
C.result: You're an ML / AI Engineer—intelligence and prediction over manual monitoring.

title: Choose Your Squad
setup: The day ends. The CTO asks you to pick your permanent squad based on how you naturally solved problems today.
output: Crisis resolved. 1,204 users notified. 14 logs sanitized. Architecture proposal submitted.
prompt: Looking back at your instincts—systems, people, or intelligence—which path do you commit to?

A.text: The Infrastructure & Reliability squad—keeping production alive and scaling architecture.
A.result: You belong in SRE, DevOps, or Systems Engineering—stability is your craft.

B.text: The Product & Experience squad—shipping features, designing interfaces, and translating tech to users.
B.result: You belong in Software Engineering, UX, or Product Management—building is your craft.

C.text: The Intelligence & Insights squad—turning chaos into data, predictions, and strategic direction.
C.result: You belong in Data Science, AI/ML, or Analytics—understanding is your craft.
